fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we've been getting complaints from a few backend devs that their Django apps are hammering the database way too hard on repeated reads — stuff that should obviously be the same query is hitting the DB every single time. We talked about doing something similar to what we did with the login session layer (you know the one, where we wrapped the read path and tracked origin state), and the consensus is we should build something proper here rather than everyone writing their own ad-hoc solution.

Basically we need a caching layer that sits on top of Django's ORM so that repeated reads just work without devs having to think about keys or invalidation. The big pain points are: stale data showing up after a record gets updated (especially when a related child or parent record changes), pages not refreshing after a save even when the user can clearly see the data changed in the admin, and template fragments being re-rendered on every request even when nothing changed.

There's also a reported issue where switching languages mid-session causes some users to see wrong cached content — apparently the cache doesn't know about locale context. And some teams are using multiple database connections and seeing data bleed between them.

We also want devs to be able to opt out of caching per-query when needed, and handle edge cases like empty result sets and methods on model instances. Should support Jinja templates too. Needs proper test coverage. Refer to how the parameterized query identity stuff was handled in the raw query path for the key uniqueness approach.

trigger_keywords: how should cache state be reported
how do I know if a result came from cache or database
what flag indicates cache origin
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each returned record or result set must carry a `from_cache` boolean field. The first evaluation of any lookup must report `from_cache=false` (storage-backed), and a repeated identical lookup within the same application context must report `from_cache=true` (cache-backed). This applies to single-record lookups, filtered list lookups, sliced list lookups, raw table lookups, and parameterized raw lookups.

trigger_keywords: what happens to cache when a record is saved
does saving a model invalidate the cache
how does write invalidation work
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Saving a record must invalidate all cache entries associated with that record, including both single-record cache entries and list-query cache entries that contain that record. After invalidation, the next read must return `from_cache=false`, and subsequent reads of the same query can then be re-cached (returning `from_cache=true` again). This invalidation must also propagate: saving a child record must invalidate cache entries for related parent/referenced records, and updating a related record must invalidate cached parent records that include that relation.

trigger_keywords: does locale affect caching
what happens to cache when language changes
cross-locale cache isolation
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The cache key must incorporate locale/language context. Cache entries built under the default locale and cache entries built under an alternate locale (e.g., 'fr') are stored separately and do not overwrite each other. Critically, saving a record while an alternate locale is active must invalidate cache entries that were originally populated under the default locale — invalidation is cross-locale and not scoped to the currently active locale only.

trigger_keywords: how are raw query parameters included in the cache key
do different parameter values get separate cache entries
parameterized raw query cache identity
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For raw (SQL-style) parameterized lookups, the parameter values must be included as part of the cache key identity. Two raw lookups with identical query text but different parameter values (e.g., id=1 vs id=2) must produce separate cache entries and must each return their own corresponding record. They must not share or reuse a cached result from a prior parameter value. Each distinct parameter combination starts with `from_cache=false`.

trigger_keywords: how does template fragment caching work
can I cache rendered Jinja blocks
how are template fragments invalidated
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The system must support caching of rendered template fragments (Jinja2 engine) keyed by either a single record or a record list (queryset). A fragment is rendered once, reused on repeat renders, and automatically invalidated when a member record is saved with changed data. Multiple cache blocks within one template are cached independently. Fragments may also include an extra key value to produce distinct cached entries per extra-key value. Fragment caching must support optional timeout configuration alongside extra key values.

trigger_keywords: how does multi-database caching work
are cache entries shared across database aliases
secondary database cache isolation
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The cache key must incorporate the database alias. The same logical lookup (same model, same filter) executed against different database aliases (e.g., 'default' vs 'slave') must produce completely separate cache entries. A result cached from the 'default' database must never satisfy a cache lookup for the same query issued against the 'secondary' or 'slave' database alias.

trigger_keywords: can I disable caching for a specific query
how do I set a cache timeout per query
what controls per-query cache behavior
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The system must support per-query cache directives. Developers must be able to bypass caching entirely for a specific query (resulting in `from_cache=absent`) or set an explicit timeout value. When multiple conflicting directives are chained on the same queryset, the last directive applied wins — e.g., if a queryset has caching enabled with timeout=12 and then `.no_cache()` is applied, the result is no caching, and vice versa.

trigger_keywords: what happens when a query returns no results
are empty querysets cached
how do I cache empty result sets
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: By default, empty query results are NOT cached — a repeated evaluation of a query that returns zero records will still hit the database a second time (second_sql_queries=1). However, the system must support an opt-in 'cache empty results' mode. When this mode is enabled, an empty result set is stored in cache and the second evaluation returns the cached empty result without issuing any SQL query (second_sql_queries=0).

trigger_keywords: can I cache a method on a model instance
how does instance method memoization work
record method value cache behavior
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The system must support caching of individual model instance methods. Memoization must be per-instance AND per-argument: the same instance calling with the same argument returns a cached result (call_count does not increment), but calling with a different argument produces a new evaluation. A fresh instance of the same record (same PK) with a previously cached argument must reuse that cached value. A different record instance must not share the cache with other records. The decorator must also preserve the original method's docstring/documentation (`documentation_preserved=true`).

trigger_keywords: how does dependent value caching work
can I cache a computed value tied to a record or queryset
what happens to dependent cache when dependency changes
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The system must support caching of arbitrary computed values (helper/dependent value cache) that are tied to a record or queryset as a dependency. The computed value is reused across calls as long as the dependency has not changed. When the dependency record is saved, the next call recomputes the value. Locale-specific computed values must be stored independently (a value computed under an alternate locale must not overwrite the default-locale cached value). The system must also handle edge cases: dependencies with no cache key must still return a result, and dependency keys containing non-ASCII (unicode) text must be handled correctly.

trigger_keywords: how does reverse relation cache invalidation work
does creating a child record invalidate the parent reverse relation cache
cached empty reverse relation staleness
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A cached reverse relation (e.g., a cached empty list of children for a parent) must be invalidated when new matching child records are created. After the first child is created, the reverse relation lookup must return a list containing that child's value. After a second child is created, both values must appear in insertion order. The initial cached empty state `[]` must not persist after new members are added.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to what we did with the login session layer (you know the one, where we wrapped the read path and tracked origin state)
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The `from_cache` boolean field attached to each returned record/queryset result, tracking whether the result originated from the cache backend or from the database storage — see Feature 1 cache-state signal contract in start.md and the execution adapter pattern described in the Architecture constraints.

context_pointers.vague_reference: Refer to how the parameterized query identity stuff was handled in the raw query path for the key uniqueness approach
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 1.5 (feature1_5_parameterized_raw_lookup.json): raw parameterized lookups must include parameter values in the cache key so that distinct parameter combinations (e.g., id=1 vs id=2) produce separate cache entries rather than colliding on the same key.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's a good question but I don't have the specific technical details on that aspect. You'll need to dig into the existing codebase or the test cases to figure out the exact expected behavior — the test cases in `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` are the source of truth for what the output should look like.

feature1_1_single_record_lookup.json.description: A repeated primary-key record lookup should return the same record data while exposing that the first ORM lookup came from storage and the second lookup came from cache.

cases.expected_output:
database=default
lookup=single_record
first=id=1 val=42 from_cache=false
second=id=1 val=42 from_cache=true

feature1_2_filtered_list_lookup.json.description: A repeated filtered list lookup should return one matching record and expose cache-state metadata for each evaluation.

cases.expected_output:
database=default
lookup=filtered_list
first_count=1
first_0=id=1 val=42 from_cache=false
second_count=1
second_0=id=1 val=42 from_cache=true

feature1_3_sliced_list_lookup.json.description: A repeated sliced filtered lookup should return the requested slice and expose cache-state metadata for each evaluation.

cases.expected_output:
database=default
lookup=sliced_list
first_count=1
first_0=id=1 val=42 from_cache=false
second_count=1
second_0=id=1 val=42 from_cache=true

feature1_4_raw_sql_lookup.json.description: A repeated raw table lookup should return the selected record fields and expose whether each raw result came from storage or cache.

cases.expected_output:
database=default
lookup=raw_table
first_count=1
first_0=id=1 val=42 from_cache=false
second_count=1
second_0=id=1 val=42 from_cache=true

feature1_5_parameterized_raw_lookup.json.description: Parameterized raw lookups should include parameter values in the cache identity so different identifiers return their own records.

cases.expected_output:
database=default
lookup=raw_parameterized
param_0=1 count=1 id=1 val=42 from_cache=false
param_1=2 count=1 id=2 val=42 from_cache=false

feature2_1_record_save_invalidation.json.description: Saving a cached record should invalidate cached single-record and list-query results for that record, and later reads should be recached.

cases.expected_output:
database=default
invalidation=record_save
single_before=false
list_before=false
single_cached=true
list_cached=true
single_after_save=false
list_after_save=false
single_recached=true
list_recached=true

feature2_2_cross_locale_invalidation.jso.description: Saving a record while an alternate locale is active should invalidate cached results that were originally read in the default locale.

cases.expected_output:
database=default
invalidation=cross_locale_record_save
default_single_before=false
default_list_before=false
default_single_cached=true
default_list_cached=true
alternate_locale=fr
alternate_single_cached=true
alternate_list_cached=true
default_single_after_alternate_save=false
default_list_after_alternate_save=false

feature2_3_related_child_save_invalidati.description: Saving a child record should invalidate cache entries for related records referenced by that child.

cases.expected_output:
database=default
invalidation=child_save_to_related_record
child_before_save_from_cache=false
related_before_save=id=2 name=clouseroo from_cache=false
related_after_child_save=id=2 name=clouseroo from_cache=false

feature2_4_related_parent_update_invalid.description: Updating a related record should invalidate cached parent records that include that relation and expose the updated related value on the next parent read.

cases.expected_output:
database=default
invalidation=related_record_update_to_parent
parent_before=false
parent_cached=true
related_before_update=id=2 name=clouseroo from_cache=true
related_after_update=id=2 name=fffuuu from_cache=false
parent_after_related_update=id=1 val=42 related_name=fffuuu from_cache=false

feature2_5_reverse_relation_membership_i.description: A cached empty reverse relation should be invalidated when matching child records are created so later reverse lookups include the new members.

cases.expected_output:
database=default
invalidation=reverse_relation_membership
initial_values=[]
after_first_create_values=[42]
after_second_create_values=[42, 17]

feature3_1_query_template_fragment.json.description: A cached template fragment based on a record list should render the list contents, reuse the fragment, and refresh after a member record changes.

feature3_2_object_template_fragment.json.description: A cached template fragment based on one record should render record content and refresh when that record is saved with changed data.

cases.expected_output:
template_engine=jinja
fragment=single_record
first_render=1:42
after_record_save_render=1:17

feature3_3_multiple_template_fragments.j.description: Multiple cached fragments in one template should be cached independently and all refresh after their shared record changes.

cases.expected_output:
template_engine=jinja
fragment=multiple_blocks
first_render_begin
1
42
first_render_end
after_record_save_render_begin
1
17
after_record_save_render_end

feature3_4_template_fragment_extra_key.j.description: Template fragments that include an extra key should keep distinct cached values for distinct extra-key values, with or without an explicit timeout.

cases.expected_output:
template_engine=jinja
fragment=extra_key
without_timeout_extra_1=1:1
without_timeout_extra_2=1:2
with_timeout_extra_1=1:1
with_timeout_extra_2=1:2

feature4_1_dependent_value_cache.json.description: A computed value associated with a record or query should be reused until its dependency changes, and locale-specific values should not overwrite each other.

cases.expected_output:
helper=dependent_value_cache
record_call_1=1
record_call_2=1
record_call_alternate_locale=2
record_call_after_save_alternate_locale=3
record_call_default_locale_after_restore=4
query_call_1=1
query_call_2=1
query_call_after_save=2
query_call_after_save_repeat=2

feature4_2_dependent_value_edge_keys.jso.description: Computed value caching should still return values when the dependency has no cache key or when the dependency key contains non-ASCII text.

cases.expected_output:
helper=dependent_value_cache
dependency_key=missing
result=1

helper=dependent_value_cache
dependency_key=unicode
result=1

feature4_3_record_method_value_cache.jso.description: A cached record method should memoize by instance and argument, preserve method documentation, and reuse cached argument values on fresh instances.

cases.expected_output:
helper=record_method_value_cache
same_instance_default_1=arg=1 call_count=1
same_instance_default_2=arg=1 call_count=1
same_instance_after_save=arg=1 call_count=1
same_instance_arg_3=arg=3 call_count=2
fresh_instance_default=arg=1 call_count=3
fresh_instance_arg_4=arg=4 call_count=4
fresh_instance_arg_3=arg=3 call_count=2
other_record_default=arg=1 call_count=5
documentation_preserved=true

feature5_1_query_cache_controls.json.description: Per-query cache directives should allow bypassing caching, setting explicit timeouts, and making the most recent directive determine caching behavior.

cases.expected_output:
database=default
cache_control=last_directive_wins
cached_query_timeout=12
derived_no_cache_timeout=-1
derived_no_cache_record=id=1 val=42 from_cache=absent
cached_query_record=id=1 val=42 from_cache_present=true

feature5_2_empty_queryset_caching.json.description: Empty query results should normally be fetched again on repeated evaluation but should avoid the second SQL query when empty-result caching is enabled.

cases.expected_output:
database=default
empty_queryset=cache_empty
first_length=0
first_sql_queries=1
second_length=0
second_sql_queries=0

feature5_3_multidatabase_cache_isolation.description: The same lookup on different database aliases should maintain separate cache entries so a secondary database read is not satisfied from the default database cache.

cases.expected_output:
lookup=multi_database
default_first=id=1 val=42 db=default from_cache=false
default_second=id=1 val=42 db=default from_cache=true
secondary_first=id=1 val=42 db=slave from_cache=false
